one_line_takeaway: TaCCS-DFA restricts cross-modal (code-sequence x code-property-graph) attention to a Fisher-information-identified task-relevant feature subspace via online Oja-estimated PCA plus adaptive gating, yielding a provably tighter noise-robustness bound and, on BigVul, a 6.3-point F1 gain (87.80% vs. 76.32% prior SOTA) over the strongest prior fusion baseline at only 3.4% extra inference latency, though absolute performance on the harder, more realistic PrimeVul benchmark remains low (best F1 25.25%).
key_finding: On BigVul (highly imbalanced, 5.0% vulnerable), TaCCS-DFA with CodeT5-Base backbone reaches F1=87.80% (P=92.31%, R=83.72%, Acc=98.11%), versus the strongest prior baseline Vul-LMGNNs(CodeT5-Base) at F1=76.32% (P=87.88%, R=67.44%) -- a 6.3-point (the paper also states 'up to 6.3 percentage points') F1 improvement over the best existing method, driven mainly by a large recall gain (67.44% -> 83.72%), which the paper translates to roughly 269 additional correctly identified vulnerable functions on BigVul's ~1,652-vulnerable-sample test set. On PrimeVul (3.0% vulnerable, hardest benchmark) TaCCS-DFA(CodeBERT) attains best F1=25.25%. On Devign (45.6% vulnerable, roughly balanced) TaCCS-DFA(CodeT5-Base) attains F1=64.60%; on ReVeal (9.9% vulnerable) F1=53.81%, both reported as best-in-table for those splits vs. the listed baselines. Ablations on BigVul/CodeBERT (Table 4) show: removing Fisher guidance (plain attention) drops F1 79.45% -> 77.78% (-2.1%, this is CodeBERT-backbone TaCCS-DFA's own ablation baseline, distinct from the CodeT5-Base headline number above); replacing the learned Fisher subspace with a random orthogonal basis drops F1 to 73.68% (-7.3%), isolating that the gain is from task-relevant geometry, not just the projection operation; removing adaptive gating drops F1 to 75.68% (-4.7%); removing Stage I contrastive alignment drops F1 to 73.42% (-7.6%); randomly rewiring 90% of CPG edges drops F1 by 20.9% to 62.86%, offered as evidence the model uses genuine graph topology rather than node features alone. Efficiency (Table 5, BigVul, batch size 64): TaCCS-DFA has 127.85M params, 2.29 sec/batch training (vs. 2.27 for Cross-Attention, +0.9%), 22.27 ms/sample inference (vs. 21.53 ms for Cross-Attention, +3.4%), 19.65 GB GPU memory (-0.1% vs. Cross-Attention), for a +10.0% F1 gain over the Cross-Attention baseline. A qualitative case study (Fig. 4, one CWE-416 UAF example) shows standard cross-attention misclassifying the sample as Safe (confidence 0.52) while Fisher-guided attention correctly flags it Vulnerable (confidence 0.94) with attention concentrated on the malloc/free/use lines (reported 170-200% weight increase on those lines) -- this is a single illustrative example, not an aggregate interpretability metric.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is TaCCS-DFA (Task-Conditioned Complementary Subspace with Dynamic Fisher Attention), a multimodal fusion framework for source-code-level vulnerability detection that fuses a pretrained-language-model encoding of Natural Code Sequence (NCS) with a Relational GCN (RGCN) encoding of a Code Property Graph (CPG). The stated novelty is three-fold, per the paper's own contribution list: (1) an empirical diagnosis on BigVul showing that naive NCS/CPG fusion suffers from information redundancy (CKA similarity of 0.68 between CodeBERT and RGCN features) and feature-extraction asymmetry (RGCN-only F1 <0.20 vs. CodeBERT-only F1 >0.63); (2) a theoretical robustness result (Theorem 1) showing that constraining cross-modal attention to a Fisher-information principal subspace of dimension k (k << d) tightens the expected output-error upper bound under isotropic input-noise perturbation from O(epsilon) for full-spectrum attention to O(sqrt(k/d)*epsilon) for their Dynamic Fisher Attention (DFA) mechanism; and (3) the TaCCS-DFA architecture itself, comprising Stage I cross-modal contrastive alignment (InfoNCE with a cross-batch memory queue) between NCS and CPG embeddings, and Stage II Dynamic Fisher Attention that estimates the Fisher principal subspace online via Oja's Rule (an incremental PCA algorithm), uses it to bias the attention query and to project/filter CPG key-value features before attention, followed by a per-sample adaptive gating unit that linearly combines the NCS CLS vector with the filtered structural vector using a learned sigmoid gate.
threat_model: Not an adversarial-attacker threat model. The paper targets a detection-accuracy/false-negative problem in the DevSecOps pipeline: vulnerable functions (in real repositories typically <5% of the codebase, per the paper's own framing) that pass through automated scanning undetected (false negatives) and enter build/release, illustrated with a canonical use-after-free (UAF) example (malloc -> conditional early use -> free -> conditional later use, CWE-416) as the running motivating case. There is no adversary model, evasion analysis, or attack surface discussed; the 'threat' is missed real vulnerabilities due to weak multimodal fusion, not an adversary manipulating the detector.
limitations: Stated explicitly in the paper's own Threats to Validity section: (1) Internal validity -- the online Oja-based Fisher subspace estimate is susceptible to gradient noise and training non-stationarity; the authors mitigate this with delayed onset of Fisher estimation, periodic updates, momentum smoothing, and orthogonalization, but this is a heuristic workaround rather than a solved problem. (2) External validity -- all four datasets are C/C++ only and the method depends on Joern-generated CPGs, so parsing failures or incomplete graphs can affect performance; despite Joern supporting Java/JavaScript/Python, the paper explicitly states multi-language and inter-procedural detection have not been validated. (3) Construct validity -- evaluation uses P/R/Acc/F1/ECE but does not account for the manual-review cost of false positives in real security auditing, which the authors flag as future work; some baseline numbers are taken from original papers rather than fully reproduced. Additional limitations evident from the paper's own tables even though not framed as such: PrimeVul F1 remains low in absolute terms (25.25% best case) despite being the 'best' among compared methods, indicating the task remains largely unsolved on high-quality/high-CWE-diversity data; recall on PrimeVul is often below 30% even for the proposed method, meaning most vulnerabilities in that harder benchmark still go undetected. The theoretical bound (Theorem 1) rests on an isotropic-noise assumption on CPG perturbations and Lipschitz continuity of the attention map -- assumptions the authors themselves note should be relaxed in future work, and the bound is a worst-case/expected perturbation-robustness argument, not a guarantee about detection accuracy or generalization.

quantitative_results: BigVul TaCCS-DFA(CodeT5-Base): P=92.31%, R=83.72%, Acc=98.11%, F1=87.80%
BigVul Vul-LMGNNs(CodeT5-Base) baseline: P=87.88%, R=67.44%, F1=76.32%
BigVul F1 improvement of proposed method over best prior baseline: 6.3 percentage points
PrimeVul TaCCS-DFA(CodeBERT): P=22.07%, R=29.51%, Acc=96.13%, F1=25.25% (best among compared methods)
Devign TaCCS-DFA(CodeT5-Base): P=57.40%, R=73.86%, Acc=62.77%, F1=64.60%
ReVeal TaCCS-DFA(CodeT5-Base): P=51.60%, R=56.22%, Acc=90.02%, F1=53.81%
BigVul GraphCodeBERT baseline: P=96.55%, R=65.12%, F1=77.78% (illustrates precision-recall imbalance in prior work)
CKA similarity between CodeBERT (NCS) and RGCN (CPG) features on BigVul: 0.68
Unimodal F1 on BigVul: RGCN(CPG)-only F1=18.50%, CodeBERT(NCS)-only F1=63.64%
Simple ConcatFusion F1 gain over CodeBERT-only on BigVul: +5.8 percentage points (69.44% vs 63.64%)

Ablation (BigVul, CodeBERT backbone, TaCCS-DFA full = F1 79.45%): w/o Fisher guidance F1=77.78% (-2.1%); w/ random Fisher basis F1=73.68% (-7.3%); w/o adaptive gating F1=75.68% (-4.7%); w/o Stage I alignment F1=73.42% (-7.6%); w/ 90% edge rewiring F1=62.86% (-20.9%)
Fisher estimation ablation: Oja (default) F1=79.45%; Direct SVD F1=75.68% (-4.7%); Power Iteration F1=72.00% (-9.4%); Randomized SVD F1=73.68% (-7.3%); Batch SVD (no EMA) F1=75.36% (-5.1%)

Efficiency (BigVul, batch 64): TaCCS-DFA 127.85M params, 2.29 sec/batch training, 22.27 ms/sample inference, 19.65 GB GPU memory, F1=79.45% (CodeBERT backbone); vs Cross-Attention baseline 2.27 sec/batch, 21.53 ms/sample, 19.67 GB, F1=72.22% -- reported as +0.9% training time, +3.4% inference latency, -0.1% memory, +10.0% F1
Theoretical robustness bound: full-spectrum attention output error <= L*epsilon; DFA expected output error <= L*sqrt(k/d)*epsilon under isotropic noise assumption
Fisher subspace energy ratio captured: 76.7%, versus random orthogonal baseline (+19.2%)

Case study (single CWE-416 UAF sample): standard attention predicts Safe (confidence 0.52, misclassified); Fisher-guided attention predicts Vulnerable (confidence 0.94); key-line attention weights increase 170-200%
baselines_compared: Few-shot LLM baselines (detailed in Appendix E, not in main tables reviewed)
